cues: Continental (Bevel 2) for serve, volley, slice, overhead — non-negotiable
Semi-Western (Bevel 4) is the modern forehand default for 4.0+ play
2H backhand: bottom hand Continental, top hand Eastern/Semi-Western FH
Grip changes happen in the non-dominant hand during the unit turn
Grip pressure: 3-4/10 during prep and backswing, 6-7/10 at contact only
Death grip (8-10/10) reduces racket head speed by 10-20%
One-grip-for-everything (Semi-Western) kills your serve, volleys, and slice
If you're fighting the racket face on any shot, move one bevel in the right direction
Contact point in front of the front hip — the single most important consistency factor
Low-to-high swing path with windshield wiper finish for topspin
Compact backswing — racket butt cap points toward the incoming ball (the slot)
Hips fire first, trunk follows, arm lags — the kinetic chain whip
3-4 feet of net clearance with topspin is the 4.5 default rally ball
Cross-court deep is the highest-percentage shot at every level
Construct points: neutral cross-court → wait for short ball → attack open court
Inside-out forehand is the most important offensive pattern at 4.5
Legs and trunk contribute 51-55% of kinetic energy — the serve is a full-body movement
Continental grip (Bevel 2) is required — forehand grip physically prevents pronation
Racket drop (power loop) is the key differentiator between advanced and beginner servers
Trophy position: elbow at shoulder height, shoulders coiled 90-110 degrees
Uncoil sequence: legs → trunk → shoulder → elbow → forearm pronation → contact
Toss flat serve: 12-18 inches in front, ~6 inches right of shoulder
If your arm feels tired after 20 serves, you're using too much arm
Pronation timing is below conscious threshold — must be trained as a motor pattern
Flat: toss in front, flush contact, maximum pronation — use on first serve when ahead
Slice: 3 o'clock contact on ball, right-to-left curve — pull opponent wide on deuce
Kick: toss above/behind head, 7-8 o'clock contact, brushes upward — default second serve
Lower body mechanics are identical across all three serve types
Kick clears net by 1-2 meters at apex — largest safety margin for second serve
Pro benchmark: first serve at ~90% max velocity, second serve at ~75% of first
Body serve wins 70-74% of points — the most underutilized target
Decide your Serve + 1 pattern before the ball toss
Split step on every single ball — the most important movement in tennis
Initiate the split step before your opponent hits, land at their moment of contact
Pros take 4-5 adjustment steps per shot; 4.0 players average just 1-2
Recovery position = bisect opponent's angle, not the center of the court
Wide balls: crossover step first, then adjustment steps to dial in spacing
At the net: shorter, wider, lower split step — cover passing shot angles
Approach shots: neutral/closed stance, continue forward after contact
Stand on the balls of your feet at all times — never flat-footed between shots
Between-point routine: positive body language → breathe → plan → ritual (20 sec total)
During rallies: Narrow-External focus — watch the ball, not your technique
"Next ball" is the single most powerful mental tool in tennis
Choking = prefrontal cortex taking over automatic motor programs — routine bypasses this
On big points: go to your most reliable serve and pattern, not your biggest
Use instructional self-talk during play ("watch the ball"), save motivational for changeovers
Label emotions to reduce amygdala activation: "I'm frustrated" cuts reactivity ~30%
Rushing after a double fault statistically worsens the next service game — reset first
Body serve wins the highest percentage (70-74%) and is the most underused target
Change highest-frequency serve target every 2-3 games — same target 3x drops win rate 8-12%
Decide Serve + 1 pattern before the ball toss, not after the ball leaves the racket
First serve percentage: 60-65% in (below 55% = too many second-serve points)
Second serve depth: back 30% of the service box — shallow invites attack
On break point: choose safer target (T/body), maintain the same speed commitment
Rushing pre-serve routine by 0.5 seconds raises double-fault rate by 22%
Quick-serve after a winner, slow-serve after a mistake — vary tempo deliberately
Tennis is primarily ATP-CP (explosive) + aerobic recovery — train both systems
Work-to-rest ratio in matches is 1:2 to 1:5 — mirror this in interval training
30/30 intervals (30 sec at 85-90%, 30 sec easy) mimic match demands precisely
Strength training can increase serve velocity 5-10% and reduce injury rates
Dynamic warm-up before every session — static stretching before play reduces power output
Don't increase weekly training volume more than 10% week over week
Deload week every 3-4 weeks: reduce volume 40-50% while maintaining intensity
Post-match: 20-30g protein + 40-60g carbs within 2 hours for recovery
Sleep is the single most powerful recovery tool — nothing else comes close
Less than 8 hours increases injury risk 1.7x (Milewski et al., 2014)
Deep sleep (N3) dominates the first half of the night — going to bed late eliminates it
Performance is most affected by sleep two nights before the event, not just the night before
Ice baths after matches: yes. Never after gym/strength workouts (blunts hypertrophy)
HRV: measure each morning — green train hard, yellow monitor, red reduce intensity
2 drinks suppress muscle protein synthesis ~24% and reduce REM sleep 9-25%
Active recovery (30-40% effort) clears lactate 3x faster than passive rest
Unit turn: shoulders and hips rotate together as one unit — not sequentially
The arm lags behind body rotation — this lag creates the whip effect
Arm should feel like it's along for the ride — not the engine
Volley: step-and-punch with a firm wrist — no backswing, no swing
Scapular stability is the platform — winged shoulder blades leak power and cause impingement
Grip pressure cycle: light in prep → firm at contact → light in follow-through
Serve and overhead use identical upper body pattern as a baseball throw
Elbow pain = grip too tight, hitting late, or excessive wrist action
Legs contribute 51-55% of kinetic energy to the serve — and significant power to groundstrokes
Knee bend 20-30 degrees for standard rally ball, 30-45 degrees for attacking forehand
Open stance: hip rotation against a braced outside leg (planted foot stays down through contact)
Closed stance: linear weight transfer — best for approach shots and short balls
Hip-shoulder separation (25-40 degrees at 4.5) stores elastic energy in the obliques
The hips fire first — front hip opens toward the net while shoulders stay sideways
Straight-leg hitting eliminates ground reaction force — all power then comes from the arm
Falling backward on serves means no forward weight transfer — land inside the baseline
The core transmits force from legs/hips to the arms — it is a drive shaft, not an engine
20% less trunk energy requires 34% more arm velocity to produce the same ball speed
Chain sequence: hips → trunk → upper arm → forearm → racket (each fires as the prior decelerates)
Firing all segments at once eliminates the multiplicative whip effect — this is the 4.0 error
At contact: head still, core braced, hitting foot planted, weight forward
Follow-through and recovery begin simultaneously — the shot flows into the next movement
After a good session where the chain works: your legs are more tired than your arm
Fix one checklist item per practice session — trying to fix everything causes paralysis
